subject: ap-psychology
title: Sensation and Perception — Sensory Adaptation, Conformity, and Pain
stimulus: Nadia is a 35-year-old nurse who works night shifts at a hospital. After years of working nights, she has noticed that she sometimes has difficulty recognizing faces of patients she has only seen once under poor lighting. She recently sought treatment for generalized anxiety disorder and was prescribed an SSRI medication by her psychiatrist. At work, Nadia has observed that new nurses on the floor tend to follow whichever senior nurse gives an instruction first, even when they later realize the instruction conflicted with hospital protocol. Nadia is patient and methodical; she prefers to complete tasks thoroughly before moving on, even if it takes longer than her colleagues expect.

parts.prompt: Explain how sensory adaptation could relate to Nadia's difficulty recognizing faces after years of working under poor lighting conditions.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that sensory adaptation involves a decrease in sensitivity to a constant, unchanging stimulus over time, and that Nadia's visual system may have adapted to low-light conditions after prolonged exposure, reducing the sensitivity of her photoreceptors in ways that make it harder to detect fine facial details needed for recognition.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how sensory adaptation relates to Nadia's face recognition difficulty. Acceptable response: Sensory adaptation is a decrease in sensitivity to a constant, unchanging stimulus over time; after years of working in poor lighting, Nadia's visual system has adapted to that level of stimulation, reducing her ability to detect the fine facial details needed for recognition.

sample_responses.response: Sensory adaptation is the tendency of our sensory systems to reduce their responsiveness to stimuli that remain constant over time. After years of working under poor hospital lighting, Nadia's visual system has adapted to those low-light conditions, meaning it no longer responds as sensitively to that level of stimulation — making it harder to detect the fine details of faces she has only seen once.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines sensory adaptation (decreased sensitivity to constant stimuli) and applies the mechanism to Nadia's prolonged exposure to poor lighting and resulting difficulty with fine facial details. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Through sensory adaptation, Nadia's visual system has become less sensitive to the low-light conditions she has been exposed to for years. This reduced sensitivity means her photoreceptors are less responsive to subtle visual cues like facial features under poor lighting, explaining her recognition difficulties.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation referencing photoreceptor sensitivity. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Sensory adaptation means that over time Nadia's brain has gotten used to poor lighting and now processes all visual information more slowly, which is why she has difficulty with faces.
sample_responses.commentary: Inaccurately describes the mechanism as slowed processing speed rather than decreased sensitivity. Sensory adaptation reduces sensitivity/responsiveness to constant stimuli — it does not slow processing speed. 0 pts.

required_elements: After years of exposure to poor lighting, Nadia's visual system has adapted to that level of stimulation, reducing her ability to pick up on fine details (such as facial features) that require good lighting to detect.
alternatives.correct_example: After years of exposure to poor lighting, Nadia's visual system has adapted to that level of stimulation, reducing her ability to pick up on fine details (such as facial features) that require good lighting to detect.

required_elements: Sensory adaptation explains why Nadia's perceptual system has become less responsive to fine visual distinctions over time due to sustained exposure to the same low-light environment.
alternatives.correct_example: Sensory adaptation explains why Nadia's perceptual system has become less responsive to fine visual distinctions over time due to sustained exposure to the same low-light environment.

parts.prompt: Explain how the role of serotonin could relate to Nadia's generalized anxiety disorder and the use of an SSRI.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that serotonin is a neurotransmitter associated with mood regulation, and that generalized anxiety disorder is linked to low serotonin activity; SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) work by blocking the reabsorption of serotonin in the synapse, increasing its availability and thereby reducing anxiety symptoms.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how serotonin relates to Nadia's GAD and SSRI treatment. Acceptable response: Serotonin is a neurotransmitter associated with mood regulation; generalized anxiety disorder is linked to low serotonin activity; SSRIs block serotonin reuptake in the synapse, increasing its availability and reducing Nadia's anxiety symptoms.

sample_responses.response: Serotonin is a neurotransmitter involved in regulating mood and anxiety. Generalized anxiety disorder is associated with insufficient serotonin activity. The SSRI Nadia was prescribed works by blocking the reuptake of serotonin into the presynaptic neuron, which keeps serotonin available in the synapse longer and increases its mood-stabilizing effect, reducing her anxiety.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies serotonin's role in mood regulation, links low activity to GAD, and explains the SSRI mechanism (reuptake inhibition). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Nadia's GAD involves dysregulation of serotonin — a neurotransmitter that helps regulate anxiety. The SSRI prevents the synapse from reabsorbing serotonin, so more remains available to reduce her anxiety symptoms.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation that correctly describes the SSRI mechanism applied to Nadia's condition. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The SSRI Nadia takes works by releasing more serotonin into the synapse, which stimulates the postsynaptic neuron and reduces anxiety.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrectly describes the mechanism. SSRIs do not release more serotonin — they block the reuptake of serotonin already in the synapse. The distinction between releasing and preventing reuptake is a common error. 0 pts.

required_elements: Low levels of serotonin activity are associated with anxiety; SSRIs increase serotonin availability in the synapse by preventing reuptake, which helps reduce Nadia's anxiety symptoms.
alternatives.correct_example: Low levels of serotonin activity are associated with anxiety; SSRIs increase serotonin availability in the synapse by preventing reuptake, which helps reduce Nadia's anxiety symptoms.

required_elements: Serotonin plays a role in regulating mood and anxiety, and the SSRI Nadia was prescribed increases serotonin availability in the brain by inhibiting its reuptake, addressing the neurochemical basis of her generalized anxiety disorder.
alternatives.correct_example: Serotonin plays a role in regulating mood and anxiety, and the SSRI Nadia was prescribed increases serotonin availability in the brain by inhibiting its reuptake, addressing the neurochemical basis of her generalized anxiety disorder.

parts.prompt: Explain how Asch's conformity research could relate to the behavior of new nurses on the floor.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Asch's research demonstrated that individuals conform to group or authority opinion even when it conflicts with what they know to be correct, and that the new nurses follow the first senior nurse's instruction even when they later realize it conflicted with protocol — a pattern consistent with conformity to social pressure.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how Asch's conformity research relates to the new nurses' behavior. Acceptable response: Asch's research showed that people conform to group or authority opinion even when it contradicts what they know to be correct; similarly, new nurses follow the senior nurse's instruction even after realizing it conflicted with hospital protocol.

sample_responses.response: Asch's conformity research demonstrated that people will go along with a group's incorrect answer due to social pressure, even when they know the answer is wrong. The new nurses' behavior mirrors this: they follow the senior nurse's instruction even when they later recognize it violated protocol — they conformed to the authority figure's direction rather than following their own knowledge of the rules.
sample_responses.commentary: Names Asch's research, describes the mechanism (conforming despite knowing the correct answer), and applies it specifically to the new nurses following incorrect instructions. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Like the participants in Asch's line experiments who gave obviously wrong answers to match the group, the new nurses defer to the senior nurse's instruction even when they later realize it was incorrect — demonstrating conformity to social pressure in a professional setting.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation that draws a direct parallel to the classic Asch line experiment. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Asch's research shows that people obey authority figures. The new nurses follow the senior nurse because they are afraid of getting in trouble if they question her instructions.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes obedience to authority (Milgram's concept) rather than conformity (Asch's concept). Asch's research is specifically about peer pressure and social conformity — changing behavior to match a group — not fear-based obedience to authority. 0 pts.

required_elements: Asch's conformity studies showed that people yield to social pressure even against their own judgment; the new nurses follow the senior nurse's instruction despite protocol conflicts in a similar way.
alternatives.correct_example: Asch's conformity studies showed that people yield to social pressure even against their own judgment; the new nurses follow the senior nurse's instruction despite protocol conflicts in a similar way.

required_elements: Like participants in Asch's line experiments who went along with an obviously incorrect group answer, the new nurses conform to the senior nurse's instruction even when they recognize it may be wrong.
alternatives.correct_example: Like participants in Asch's line experiments who went along with an obviously incorrect group answer, the new nurses conform to the senior nurse's instruction even when they recognize it may be wrong.

parts.prompt: Explain how the primacy effect could relate to the new nurses' tendency to follow whichever senior nurse speaks first.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the primacy effect refers to the tendency to better remember and be more influenced by information presented first in a series, and that the new nurses are more influenced by the instruction given first because of the primacy effect — the first piece of information they received made a stronger impression.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the primacy effect relates to the new nurses' tendency to follow whichever senior nurse speaks first. Acceptable response: The primacy effect is the tendency for information presented first to be remembered and weighted more heavily; new nurses follow the first senior nurse's instruction because that first piece of information made a stronger impression in memory.

sample_responses.response: The primacy effect is the tendency to remember and be more influenced by information presented early in a sequence. New nurses tend to follow the first senior nurse who gives an instruction because that first piece of information is encoded more strongly in memory, giving it more weight in their decision about how to act.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines the primacy effect and explains why information presented first carries disproportionate influence — applied directly to the nurses' behavior. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Because of the primacy effect, the first instruction the new nurses hear gets encoded more strongly, making it more memorable and influential. When later instructions conflict with the first one, the nurses default to the first because it had a stronger initial impression.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation emphasizing the encoding advantage of early information and its persistence under later conflicting input. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The primacy effect means people pay more attention to the most recent information they receive. The new nurses follow whoever spoke last because that is most fresh in their minds.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the recency effect, not the primacy effect. The primacy effect refers to the superior memory for and influence of information presented first — the opposite of what this response describes. 0 pts.

required_elements: The primacy effect explains the new nurses' behavior: the first instruction they heard had a disproportionate influence on their actions because information presented first tends to be remembered and weighted more heavily.
alternatives.correct_example: The primacy effect explains the new nurses' behavior: the first instruction they heard had a disproportionate influence on their actions because information presented first tends to be remembered and weighted more heavily.

required_elements: The new nurses defer to whichever senior nurse spoke first because of the primacy effect — earlier-presented information tends to carry more weight in memory and decision-making.
alternatives.correct_example: The new nurses defer to whichever senior nurse spoke first because of the primacy effect — earlier-presented information tends to carry more weight in memory and decision-making.

parts.prompt: Explain how the conscientiousness dimension of the Big Five personality traits could relate to Nadia's work style.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that conscientiousness involves being organized, dependable, self-disciplined, and thorough, and that Nadia's preference for completing tasks fully before moving on reflects a high level of conscientiousness.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the conscientiousness dimension relates to Nadia's work style. Acceptable response: Conscientiousness is characterized by organization, diligence, thoroughness, and self-discipline; Nadia's patient, methodical approach to completing tasks fully before moving on reflects high conscientiousness.

sample_responses.response: Conscientiousness is one of the Big Five personality traits characterized by being organized, diligent, thorough, and self-disciplined. Nadia's preference for completing tasks fully before moving on — even when it takes longer than colleagues expect — reflects a high level of conscientiousness.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines conscientiousness using its key descriptors and applies them accurately to Nadia's work behavior. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Nadia's methodical, thorough approach to her nursing tasks is consistent with high conscientiousness in the Big Five model. Conscientious individuals prioritize doing work carefully and completely over speed — exactly what Nadia demonstrates.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation applying the conscientiousness concept to Nadia's specific behavior pattern. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Nadia's personality reflects high agreeableness because she is patient with patients and colleagues even when they expect faster work.
sample_responses.commentary: Names the wrong Big Five trait. Agreeableness refers to warmth, cooperation, and trust in social relationships — not thoroughness or task completion. Nadia's behavior (completing tasks fully, being methodical) is specific to conscientiousness. 0 pts.

required_elements: Nadia's methodical and thorough approach to completing tasks before moving on is consistent with high conscientiousness — a Big Five trait characterized by diligence, organization, and attention to detail.
alternatives.correct_example: Nadia's methodical and thorough approach to completing tasks before moving on is consistent with high conscientiousness — a Big Five trait characterized by diligence, organization, and attention to detail.

required_elements: Conscientiousness describes Nadia's work style: being patient, careful, and thorough are core features of high conscientiousness in the Big Five model.
alternatives.correct_example: Conscientiousness describes Nadia's work style: being patient, careful, and thorough are core features of high conscientiousness in the Big Five model.

parts.prompt: Explain how Piaget's concept of accommodation could relate to how new nurses adjust their mental framework after discovering that a senior nurse's instruction conflicted with hospital protocol.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that accommodation involves modifying an existing schema to incorporate new information that does not fit the existing framework, and that when new nurses discover that the senior nurse's instruction conflicted with protocol, they must accommodate by revising their schema about what correct nursing practice looks like.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how Piaget's accommodation relates to new nurses adjusting after the protocol conflict. Acceptable response: Accommodation involves modifying an existing schema to fit new information; when new nurses discover the senior nurse's instruction conflicted with protocol, they must accommodate by revising their schema about the reliability of senior nurse instructions or what correct practice involves.

sample_responses.response: Piaget's accommodation occurs when existing schemas cannot assimilate new information and must be modified. When new nurses discover that the senior nurse's instruction violated hospital protocol, they cannot fit this new information into their existing schema that 'senior nurses are always correct' — so they must accommodate by modifying that schema to recognize that even experienced nurses can give incorrect instructions.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines accommodation, identifies the old schema that needs modification, and explains the specific update required by the new contradictory information. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Accommodation means adjusting a mental framework to include information that doesn't fit. After discovering the protocol conflict, the new nurses must revise their schema about how to respond to senior nurse instructions — updating their understanding that instructions should be verified against protocol, not just followed automatically.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path emphasizing the schema revision about protocol verification. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Piaget's accommodation means the new nurses would fit the senior nurse's instruction into their existing understanding of nursing practice without changing anything about their beliefs.
sample_responses.commentary: This describes assimilation, not accommodation. Assimilation is fitting new information into existing schemas without changing them; accommodation is the modification of schemas to handle information that does not fit. 0 pts.

required_elements: When new nurses realize the instruction conflicted with protocol, they must accommodate — adjusting their existing mental framework about the senior nurse's authority or accuracy to incorporate this new contradictory information.
alternatives.correct_example: When new nurses realize the instruction conflicted with protocol, they must accommodate — adjusting their existing mental framework about the senior nurse's authority or accuracy to incorporate this new contradictory information.

required_elements: Accommodation occurs when existing schemas must be altered to fit new information; the new nurses update their understanding of how to follow instructions when they learn that the senior nurse's guidance can conflict with official protocol.
alternatives.correct_example: Accommodation occurs when existing schemas must be altered to fit new information; the new nurses update their understanding of how to follow instructions when they learn that the senior nurse's guidance can conflict with official protocol.

parts.prompt: Explain how the gate-control theory of pain could relate to a technique Nadia might use to help a patient manage discomfort during a procedure.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that gate-control theory proposes that the spinal cord contains a 'gate' mechanism that can block or allow pain signals to pass to the brain, and that non-painful stimulation (such as touch, rubbing, or distraction) can close this gate and reduce perceived pain, meaning Nadia could use techniques like rubbing the area near an injection site or using distraction to reduce a patient's pain perception.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how gate-control theory relates to a pain management technique Nadia might use. Acceptable response: Gate-control theory proposes a spinal gate that can be closed by competing sensory input or cognitive factors; Nadia could reduce a patient's pain by providing gentle tactile stimulation near the procedure site or using distraction to close the gate and reduce pain signals reaching the brain.

sample_responses.response: Gate-control theory proposes that the spinal cord has a neural 'gate' that can either allow or block pain signals from reaching the brain. Competing sensory input — like gentle rubbing near an injection site — can close this gate, reducing the pain the patient perceives. Nadia could rub the skin around the needle site before an injection to activate touch fibers that close the gate and minimize the patient's pain experience.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly describes the gate-control mechanism (spinal gate, competing sensory input), identifies a specific applicable technique (rubbing the injection site), and explains how it reduces pain perception. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: According to gate-control theory, the spinal cord can block pain signals from reaching the brain when the gate is closed. Nadia could use distraction — asking the patient to focus on something other than the procedure — which can engage cognitive pathways that close the gate and reduce perceived pain intensity.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid path using distraction (a cognitive gate-closing technique). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Gate-control theory means Nadia should give the patient pain medication before the procedure so that the gate in the brain is blocked and less pain is felt.
sample_responses.commentary: Gate-control theory describes a spinal (not brain) mechanism activated by non-pharmacological competing stimulation or cognitive factors — it does not describe the mechanism of analgesic drugs. This response conflates two separate pain-reduction approaches. 0 pts.

required_elements: Gate-control theory predicts that Nadia could reduce a patient's pain by providing competing sensory stimulation (such as gentle rubbing) near the painful site, which closes the spinal gate and reduces pain signals reaching the brain.
alternatives.correct_example: Gate-control theory predicts that Nadia could reduce a patient's pain by providing competing sensory stimulation (such as gentle rubbing) near the painful site, which closes the spinal gate and reduces pain signals reaching the brain.

required_elements: Nadia could apply gate-control theory by using distraction or gentle tactile stimulation to close the neural 'gate' in the spinal cord, reducing the intensity of pain signals the patient perceives during a procedure.
alternatives.correct_example: Nadia could apply gate-control theory by using distraction or gentle tactile stimulation to close the neural 'gate' in the spinal cord, reducing the intensity of pain signals the patient perceives during a procedure.
